questions.question: What is the main purpose of a full mock loop?
options: To practice sequencing, timing, and judgment across the whole interview rather than polishing one isolated subsystem.
To memorize a single architecture that can be reused unchanged.
To avoid redesign follow-ups until the final lesson.
To focus only on generating large diagrams quickly.
questions.explanation: A full mock tests flow, prioritization, and adaptation under time pressure.

questions.question: Why should a full mock include a redesign step?
options: Because strong answers should show how changed constraints alter decisions instead of pretending the first design was universally optimal.
Because redesign replaces the need for a high-level architecture.
Because interviewers always reveal the final answer during redesign.
Because redesign is mainly for frontend prompts.
questions.explanation: Redesign exposes whether the original design was grounded in clear assumptions and trade-offs.

questions.question: What is the strongest signal that the mock answer stayed well structured?
options: It moved from scope to sizing to architecture to a deliberate deep dive before closing with risks and redesign.
It started with implementation details to save time.
It avoided quantification so the conversation stayed flexible.
It kept all stages open at once instead of sequencing them.
questions.explanation: A disciplined sequence is one of the biggest differences between average and strong performances.

questions.question: What is the main danger of spending too long in clarification?
options: The answer can lose time needed for actual design, depth, and wrap-up without gaining enough new information.
It automatically forces eventual consistency.
It prevents any future deep dive entirely.
It matters only for short prompts like URL shortener.
questions.explanation: Clarification is important, but it must unlock progress rather than replace it.

questions.question: Which trade-off usually comes with choosing one strong deep dive in a mock?
options: You show more judgment and credibility in one area, but some secondary topics will receive less airtime.
You eliminate the need for high-level architecture.
You guarantee a perfect answer regardless of workload estimates.
You make observability unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Depth improves signal, but it uses time that could have gone elsewhere.

questions.question: Which metric is most useful for reviewing a mock performance afterward?
options: Whether each phase of the answer happened at all and whether at least one estimate and one detection signal were included.
The number of technologies mentioned.
How many pages of notes were produced.
Whether the candidate used a preferred buzzword in the first five minutes.
questions.explanation: Post-mock review should focus on structure and signal, not ornamentation.

questions.question: If the interviewer changes the top priority late in the mock, what is the best response?
options: Restate which assumption changed, identify the affected components, and explain the new trade-off landscape clearly.
Keep the first design unchanged to maintain confidence.
Ignore the change unless it affects APIs directly.
Throw away the earlier answer and begin a new one with unrelated assumptions.
questions.explanation: A strong redesign reuses earlier reasoning while updating the affected decisions.

questions.question: What most strongly distinguishes a full mock from isolated lesson practice?
options: The mock tests transitions between stages, time management, and recovery from follow-up pressure as much as individual concepts.
The mock uses only one technology stack.
The mock avoids any operational concerns so the pace stays high.
The mock is mainly about code rather than design.
questions.explanation: The full loop is about joining all the skills into one coherent interview performance.
